depthLevels.label: Intro
depthLevels.description: Understand the core idea and vocabulary.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can explain the concept in plain language.

depthLevels.label: Guided Practice
depthLevels.description: Apply the concept with examples and hints.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can solve similar problems with light guidance.

depthLevels.label: Applied
depthLevels.description: Use the concept in realistic scenarios.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can use the concept in project-like situations.

depthLevels.label: Mastery
depthLevels.description: Connect this concept to architecture and trade-offs.
depthLevels.expectedOutcome: Can teach and adapt the concept to new contexts.

concepts.title: Service Facade Pattern
concepts.summary: Encapsulate data access and orchestration behind a small API for UI layers.

relatedTopics.note: Understand constructor/inject usage first.
Signals provide ergonomic local state in facades.
Facades become more useful when HTTP concerns are standardized.

examples.title: Restaurant analogy
examples.prompt: Why should a component call a facade instead of HttpClient directly?
examples.solution: The component is the diner, facade is the waiter, and HttpClient is the kitchen. The diner should not walk into the kitchen.

examples.title: Typed facade API
examples.prompt: Create a method that returns strongly typed concepts from local JSON.
examples.solution: Use HttpClient.get<LearningContent>() and expose computed/signal selectors.

examples.title: Refactor component orchestration
examples.prompt: Move loading/error/empty-state logic from a component into a facade. Add one public load() API and at least two computed selectors.
examples.solution: Extract imperative orchestration into facade methods, keep view layer declarative, and expose readonly signals for rendering states.

concepts.title: Signals for Local Reactive State
concepts.summary: Use Angular signals to keep local state predictable, composable, and side-effect aware.

relatedTopics.note: Facade state can be expressed as signals.
Use interop utilities to bridge observables and signals.

examples.title: Filter by depth
examples.prompt: Implement a computed signal that returns concepts available at a selected depth level.
examples.solution: Use selectedDepth signal and concepts signal with computed(() => concepts().filter(...)).

concepts.title: Dependency Injection and Token Design
concepts.summary: Use DI tokens and provider scopes to swap implementations and isolate side effects.

relatedTopics.note: Facades depend on DI for transport and repositories.
Provider scoping is central in standalone apps.

examples.title: Swap data source
examples.prompt: How can DI help replace mock JSON with a real backend later?
examples.solution: Depend on an abstract repository token and provide a different implementation by environment.

examples.title: Create a repository token
examples.prompt: Define an InjectionToken<LearningRepository> and provide one in-memory and one HTTP implementation. Toggle them by environment provider config.
examples.solution: Expose shared interface, register useClass/useFactory providers, and inject token in facade without touching component code.

concepts.title: Standalone Architecture and Feature Boundaries
concepts.summary: Organize apps using standalone components, route-level providers, and explicit feature boundaries.

relatedTopics.note: Standalone route declarations simplify lazy loading.
You need provider scope fundamentals first.

examples.title: Feature route providers
examples.prompt: Define a standalone route that provides a feature-scoped facade and API base URL.
examples.solution: Use a route with providers: [FeatureFacade, { provide: API_URL, useValue: ... }].

examples.title: Extract a bounded feature
examples.prompt: Take one existing page and split it into a standalone feature with its own routes, providers, and shared public barrel.
examples.solution: Move implementation details into feature folder, expose only route and public types, and verify imports remain one-way.

concepts.title: RxJS + Signals Interop
concepts.summary: Combine stream-based workflows with signal-driven UI state without double subscriptions.

relatedTopics.note: Start from signal fundamentals.
HTTP streams often feed UI signals.

examples.title: Observable pipeline to signal
examples.prompt: You have a debounced search Observable. How do you expose results as signal state in a component?
examples.solution: Use toSignal(searchResults$) for read path, keep side effects in RxJS pipeline, and avoid duplicate local subscribe().

examples.title: Bi-directional bridge
examples.prompt: Create a search box where user input is a signal but API calls are RxJS. Add cancellation and loading state.
examples.solution: Convert signal to observable for switchMap, then map final stream back to signal via toSignal.

concepts.title: Route-Level Code Splitting and Prefetch Strategy
concepts.summary: Use lazy routes, deferrable chunks, and selective prefetching to balance performance and UX.

relatedTopics.note: Standalone routes make split points explicit.
Deferring non-critical blocks complements route splitting.

examples.title: Lazy route with preload hint
examples.prompt: Add a lazy feature route and mark it as prefetch candidate after first interaction.
examples.solution: Use loadComponent/loadChildren and metadata consumed by a custom preloading strategy.

examples.title: Measure and tune
examples.prompt: Split one heavy route into separate chunks and measure initial bundle delta before/after. Document any regressions.
examples.solution: Record baseline bundle stats, apply lazy boundaries, compare startup TTI/LCP and adjust preloading accordingly.

concepts.title: Deferrable Views for Progressive Rendering
concepts.summary: Render critical UI first and defer secondary blocks using trigger-based loading.

relatedTopics.note: Both reduce initial JavaScript pressure.
Signals help model loaded/deferred states in templates.

examples.title: Magazine fold analogy
examples.prompt: Why defer below-the-fold content?
examples.solution: You print the cover first so users can start reading immediately; long articles can arrive a moment later.

examples.title: Add defer blocks
examples.prompt: Wrap a non-critical widget in @defer with loading and placeholder states. Use viewport or idle trigger and compare perceived speed.
examples.solution: Implement @defer with @placeholder/@loading, verify main content paints first, and ensure deferred state remains accessible.

concepts.title: Zoneless Change Detection with Signal-First Patterns
concepts.summary: Design deterministic UI updates without Zone.js by leaning on explicit reactivity.

relatedTopics.note: Signals are the primary update mechanism in zoneless setups.
Interop avoids hidden async change propagation.

examples.title: Event-driven updates
examples.prompt: A component updates after async calls only. What must be explicit when Zone.js is removed?
examples.solution: All state transitions must flow through reactive primitives/signals observed by the template, with no reliance on patched async APIs.

examples.title: Migrate one feature
examples.prompt: Pick one feature and remove implicit mutation patterns. Convert state to signals/computed and verify UI updates still occur deterministically.
examples.solution: Replace mutable local fields with signal state, isolate effects, and ensure template reads only reactive values.

concepts.title: Dynamic Component Composition
concepts.summary: Compose runtime UIs with typed contracts for plugin-like sections and context-aware rendering.

relatedTopics.note: Standalone components simplify dynamic registration.
Injection tokens can register dynamic blocks safely.

examples.title: Typed dynamic outlet
examples.prompt: Define a typed config model that maps widget IDs to component classes and input schemas.
examples.solution: Use a discriminated union for widget config and strongly typed component input contracts.

examples.title: Add pluggable section
examples.prompt: Implement a dashboard section registry where adding a new widget requires no dashboard template edits.
examples.solution: Introduce registry token, iterate over configs, and render via ngComponentOutlet/dynamic APIs with validated inputs.

concepts.title: Resilient HTTP Data Layer
concepts.summary: Centralize retries, timeout, mapping, and error normalization to keep UI logic clean and robust.

relatedTopics.note: Facades consume normalized data-layer outputs.
Stream operators power retries, fallback, and cancellation.

examples.title: Normalize API errors
examples.prompt: Wrap HttpClient errors into a domain-safe Result type with retry/backoff for idempotent requests.
examples.solution: Use pipe(catchError, retryWhen/backoff) and map to a typed Result union consumed by facades.

examples.title: Design request policy
examples.prompt: Implement one interceptor or data-access utility that applies timeout, correlation ID, and user-friendly error mapping for all feature APIs.
examples.solution: Create shared policy layer, expose typed success/error contracts, and ensure UI receives predictable failure states.

concepts.title: Observer Pattern in Angular
concepts.summary: The Observer pattern is foundational in Angular, implemented via RxJS Observables, EventEmitter, and modern Signals.

examples.title: Newspaper Subscription
examples.prompt: How does the Observer pattern work in a real-world scenario?
examples.solution: A newspaper publisher (Subject) sends updates to all subscribers (Observers) whenever a new edition is ready. Subscribers don't check the mailbox every minute; they are notified when the paper arrives.

examples.title: Signal-based Observer
examples.prompt: Implement a shared state service using a Signal that multiple components 'observe' and react to using effect().
examples.solution: Create a service with a private writable signal and a public readonly signal. Use effect(() => { ... }) in components to react to changes.

concepts.title: Strategy Pattern via DI
concepts.summary: Decouple algorithms from their usage by swapping implementations through Angular's Dependency Injection system.

examples.title: Interface Swapping
examples.prompt: Define a Logger interface and provide different implementations (ConsoleLogger vs NoOpLogger) using DI tokens.
examples.solution: export abstract class Logger { abstract log(msg: string): void; } ... providers: [{ provide: Logger, useClass: isProd ? NoOpLogger : ConsoleLogger }]

concepts.title: Decorator Pattern in Directives
concepts.summary: Extend or modify the behavior of elements or components without changing their source code using Directives and Interceptors.

examples.title: Tooltip Directive
examples.prompt: Create a directive that 'decorates' an HTML element by adding a hover-triggered tooltip without modifying the host component.
examples.solution: Use @Directive({ selector: '[appTooltip]' }) and HostListener to manage visibility of a dynamically created tooltip element.

concepts.title: Factory Pattern with useFactory
concepts.summary: Centralize object creation logic, especially when initialization depends on runtime conditions or other dependencies.

examples.title: Conditional Provider
examples.prompt: Use useFactory to provide a different configuration service based on a 'runtime-feature-flag' signal or cookie.
examples.solution: { provide: CONFIG, useFactory: (flag) => flag ? new NewConfig() : new OldConfig(), deps: [FEATURE_FLAG_TOKEN] }

concepts.title: Singleton Pattern (providedIn: 'root')
concepts.summary: Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access, managed natively by Angular's root injector.

examples.title: Single Source of Truth
examples.prompt: Why use providedIn: 'root'?
examples.solution: It's like a building's main water supply. There is only one pump, and everyone in the building gets water from that same source, ensuring consistent pressure (state) for everyone.

concepts.title: Proxy Pattern via Interceptors
concepts.summary: Control access to a resource (like an API) by intercepting requests/responses to add logging, auth, or caching.

examples.title: Auth Interceptor
examples.prompt: Implement an interceptor that 'proxies' every HTTP request to add an Authorization header.
examples.solution: intercept(req, next) { const authReq = req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: 'Bearer ' + token } }); return next(authReq); }

concepts.title: Template Method Pattern
concepts.summary: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in an abstract base class, letting subclasses override specific steps (like lifecycle hooks).

examples.title: Abstract Base List
examples.prompt: Create an abstract BaseListComponent that defines a refresh() template method calling an abstract fetchData() method.
examples.solution: Subclasses implement fetchData(), while BaseListComponent handles the 'refresh' workflow (loading state, error handling, etc.).
